refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement period and baseline, (2) what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable' (e.g., does safety shutdown count as unavailability?), (3) which subsystems or failure modes dominate unavailability, and (4) the allocation of availability targets across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. High safety criticality means that safety-driven shutdowns must be distinguished from reliability failures.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure modes, maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Unplanned Downtime − Planned Maintenance) / Total Time. Compare against 0.97 threshold. Stratify by subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) to identify bottlenecks.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall achieve ≥0.98 availability to account for mechanical and propulsion reliability.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability to ensure remote control link stability and latency compliance.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability to ensure continuous hazard detection and emergency shutdown capability.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive maintenance and fault diagnostics to minimize unplanned downtime.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant subsystems (dual Vehicle units, dual Teleoperation links, dual Safety Services channels) with automatic failover to achieve 0.97+ availability through N+1 redundancy.

DP-B: Implement high-reliability component selection, rigorous preventive maintenance scheduling, and real-time health monitoring with predictive diagnostics to achieve 0.97+ availability through MTBF/MTTR optimization.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach combining selective redundancy (critical paths only: Teleoperation link, Safety Services) with high-reliability components and predictive maintenance for cost-effective 0.97+ availability.
DP-D: Implement modular hot-swap architecture allowing rapid component replacement without system shutdown, combined with spare parts inventory management and on-site technician support.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hybrid availability strategy combining: (1) redundant Teleoperation network links (dual WAN/LTE paths) with automatic failover, (2) redundant Safety Services channels (dual hazard detection and emergency shutdown paths), (3) high-reliability Vehicle component selection (MTBF ≥5000 hrs for critical drivetrain/hydraulics), (4) predictive maintenance via real-time telemetry monitoring (vibration, temperature, pressure sensors), and (5) scheduled preventive maintenance intervals optimized to minimize unplanned downtime.

potential_sources: Teleoperation redundancy couples to Network Link interface (requires dual-path capability).
Safety Services redundancy couples to SafetyAlert interface (requires dual-channel hazard reporting).
Predictive maintenance couples to Telemetry interface (requires continuous sensor data streaming).
Vehicle component selection couples to maintenance logistics (spare parts availability, technician training).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids full system redundancy (cost-prohibitive) while targeting high-risk failure modes (Teleoperation loss, Safety Services failure). Coupling is manageable if Network Link and SafetyAlert interfaces support dual-channel operation. Predictive maintenance requires robust Telemetry infrastructure.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer accepts hybrid redundancy model (not full N+1 across all subsystems).
ASSUMPTION: Network infrastructure (WAN/LTE) can support dual-path Teleoperation with <100ms latency on both paths.
ASSUMPTION: Safety Services can be architected with dual-channel hazard detection without race conditions or common-mode failures.
RISK: Predictive maintenance effectiveness depends on sensor calibration and algorithm accuracy; poor diagnostics could mask failures.
RISK: Spare parts supply chain delays could extend MTTR beyond acceptable limits.
RISK: Operator training on failover procedures may be incomplete, reducing effective availability during transitions.
RISK: Coupling between Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert interfaces could create bottlenecks if not carefully managed.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) failover detection time and switchover latency for Teleoperation redundancy, (2) Safety Services dual-channel architecture (e.g., voting logic, cross-check mechanisms), (3) predictive maintenance thresholds and alert escalation procedures, (4) spare parts inventory targets and supply chain SLAs, and (5) operator training and certification requirements for failover scenarios.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates availability across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the overall availability KPI, the predictive maintenance framework, the failover coordination logic, and the integration of Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound sensor data (vibration, temperature, pressure, operational state) from Vehicle and Safety Services for predictive maintenance analysis and availability monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Inbound operational commands from Site Manager and Tele-operator; NGHE Solution must ensure Tasking is only accepted when availability conditions are met (e.g., redundant Teleoperation link is active).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety alerts to Safety Manager and emergency shutdown signals; NGHE Solution must ensure SafetyAlert is always available (dual-channel) regardless of other subsystem failures.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network connectivity for Teleoperation and Telemetry; NGHE Solution must manage dual-path failover and ensure Network Link redundancy is transparent to operators.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the availability KPI. It contains the predictive maintenance framework, failover coordination, and interface orchestration. Minimal diffusion: only the implementation of redundancy logic in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems is delegated.

Vehicle owns component-level reliability (MTBF, MTTR) but does not own the overall availability KPI or failover logic. Assigning availability to Vehicle alone would diffuse responsibility and obscure the system-level redundancy strategy.

Teleoperation owns the dual-link redundancy and failover for remote control, but does not own the overall availability KPI or the integration with Safety Services and Vehicle. Partial containment only.
Safety Services owns dual-channel hazard detection and emergency shutdown, but does not own the overall availability KPI or the integration with Vehicle and Teleoperation. Partial containment only.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the correct owner of CR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI) because: (1) availability is a system-level property that emerges from the interaction of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (2) the predictive maintenance framework and failover coordination logic must be centralized to avoid conflicts and ensure coherent decision-making; (3) the integration of Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces is inherently a system-level concern; (4) the availability target (0.97) is a customer-facing SLA that cannot be delegated to subsystems without risking misalignment.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution module is too abstract or lacks clear ownership, availability responsibilities could diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, leading to finger-pointing and accountability gaps.
RISK: Predictive maintenance framework may require deep integration with Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; if not carefully architected, this could create tight coupling and reduce modularity.
RISK: Failover coordination logic must be deterministic and testable; if implemented as ad-hoc patches across subsystems, it could become fragile and error-prone.
RISK: Network Link redundancy (dual-path failover) may introduce latency or jitter that violates Teleoperation control loop requirements; this must be validated early.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish a clear NGHE Solution module charter that explicitly owns: (1) the availability KPI and measurement methodology, (2) the predictive maintenance framework (sensor integration, algorithm, alert escalation), (3) the failover coordination logic (detection, switchover, recovery), (4) the interface orchestration (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link), and (5) the integration testing and validation of redundancy. Delegate to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services the implementation of their respective reliability targets and redundancy mechanisms, but require them to report health status and failover readiness to NGHE Solution via standardized interfaces.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid redundancy + predictive maintenance) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP is specific enough to guide design (dual Teleoperation links, dual Safety Services channels, high-reliability Vehicle components, predictive maintenance) while remaining flexible enough to accommodate implementation details. The coupling check identified manageable dependencies on Network Link and SafetyAlert interfaces. Assumptions and risks are documented and actionable.

diffusion_risks: Predictive maintenance framework implementation may diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services if not centralized in NGHE Solution.
Failover coordination logic could be scattered across subsystems if ownership boundaries are not clearly defined.
Spare parts inventory management and supply chain coordination may require cross-module collaboration, risking accountability gaps.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for the availability KPI. It owns the system-level orchestration, predictive maintenance framework, and failover logic. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are delegated to implement their respective reliability targets and redundancy mechanisms, but must report health status to NGHE Solution. This containment model minimizes diffusion while preserving modularity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-004 is feasible via operational telemetry collection and availability calculation over the agreed evaluation period. Predictive maintenance effectiveness can be validated by comparing predicted failures to actual failures. Failover mechanisms can be tested via controlled fault injection. Network Link redundancy can be validated via dual-path latency and jitter measurements.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The refined FR clearly allocates availability targets to Vehicle (≥0.98), Teleoperation (≥0.99), and Safety Services (≥0.99). These sub-FRs can be traced to specific design parameters (component selection, redundancy, maintenance) and implementation modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). The allocation is mathematically sound (combined availability ≥ 0.98 × 0.99 × 0.99 ≈ 0.97).

recommended_revisions: Refine the predictive maintenance framework specification to include sensor types, data collection frequency, algorithm details, and alert thresholds.
Define failover detection time and switchover latency requirements for Teleoperation redundancy (e.g., <5 seconds for detection, <2 seconds for switchover).
Specify Safety Services dual-channel architecture (e.g., voting logic, cross-check mechanisms) to ensure no common-mode failures.
Establish spare parts inventory targets and supply chain SLAs to ensure MTTR is within acceptable limits.
Define operator training and certification requirements for failover scenarios and emergency procedures.
Validate Network Link redundancy design against Teleoperation control loop latency and jitter requirements.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into sub-FRs targeting specific subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and failure modes (mechanical reliability, network availability, hazard detection). The refined FR and selected DP are sound, but the next level of decomposition is necessary to allocate availability targets, define redundancy mechanisms, and establish predictive maintenance requirements. Decomposition will enable detailed design of Vehicle reliability, Teleoperation failover, Safety Services dual-channel architecture, and predictive maintenance framework.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs: FR-NGHE-004.1 (Vehicle availability), FR-NGHE-004.2 (Teleoperation availability), FR-NGHE-004.3 (Safety Services availability), and FR-NGHE-004.4 (predictive maintenance). Each sub-FR requires its own FR→DP→IM decomposition to guide detailed design. For example, FR-NGHE-004.2 (Teleoperation ≥0.99 availability) will decompose into DPs for dual-link redundancy, failover detection, and switchover logic, assigned to the Teleoperation module. Similarly, FR-NGHE-004.4 (predictive maintenance) will decompose into DPs for sensor integration, data collection, algorithm design, and alert escalation, assigned to NGHE Solution.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall achieve ≥0.98 availability through high-reliability component selection (MTBF ≥5000 hrs for critical drivetrain/hydraulics) and preventive maintenance scheduling.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability through redundant network links (dual WAN/LTE paths) with automatic failover and <5 second detection time.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability through dual-channel hazard detection and emergency shutdown with voting logic and cross-checks.

FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive maintenance via real-time telemetry monitoring (vibration, temperature, pressure sensors) with anomaly detection and alert escalation to minimize unplanned downtime.

assumptions: Customer accepts hybrid redundancy model (not full N+1 across all subsystems).
Network infrastructure (WAN/LTE) can support dual-path Teleoperation with <100ms latency on both paths.
Safety Services can be architected with dual-channel hazard detection without race conditions or common-mode failures.
Predictive maintenance algorithms can achieve >90% accuracy in failure prediction.
Spare parts supply chain can meet MTTR targets (e.g., <24 hours for critical components).
Operators can be trained and certified on failover procedures and emergency shutdown.
Telemetry interface can support continuous sensor data streaming at required frequency and latency.
open_risks: Predictive maintenance effectiveness depends on sensor calibration and algorithm accuracy; poor diagnostics could mask failures.
Spare parts supply chain delays could extend MTTR beyond acceptable limits.
Operator training on failover procedures may be incomplete, reducing effective availability during transitions.
Network Link redundancy (dual-path failover) may introduce latency or jitter that violates Teleoperation control loop requirements.
Safety Services dual-channel architecture could introduce race conditions or common-mode failures if not carefully designed.
Coupling between Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert interfaces could create bottlenecks if not carefully managed.
Failover coordination logic could become fragile and error-prone if implemented as ad-hoc patches across subsystems.
evidence_needed: Detailed specification of predictive maintenance framework (sensor types, data collection frequency, algorithm, thresholds).
Failover detection time and switchover latency measurements for Teleoperation redundancy.
Safety Services dual-channel architecture design (voting logic, cross-check mechanisms, FMEA).
Spare parts inventory targets and supply chain SLAs.
Operator training and certification plan for failover scenarios.
Network Link redundancy validation against Teleoperation control loop requirements (latency, jitter, throughput).
Operational telemetry data from pilot deployments to validate availability calculations and predictive maintenance effectiveness.
Customer acceptance criteria and measurement methodology for the 0.97 availability target.
